speaker: interviewer
text: OK, John, getting up speed. And I'm recording slate.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Mr. Joseph Brod on March 15th, 1996 in San Diego, California. My name is John Kent. And we'll do this in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting up to speed. And you can start.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's start with spelling your name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Brod. B-R-O-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a different original name, Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, it was original, because it's a derivative, as I told you before, that those names were-- Jews, especially in the monarchy, Bohemian and so, they were forced-- only the oldest son in a family was permitted to marry legally and stay in the town as a denizen-- not citizen, denizen. But the youngest son, if he married here, to live and look for some other town  or village that would accept him as a denizen [? and ?] [? innocent. ?] So Brod-- what it meant there, ford. Like in Poland, brod means also a ford over a river. So they came from some town over-- on a ford.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had only first names. So the new neighbors to distinguish them, they said, oh, he's from the brod. Then it became Brod. That's what it means. And what is your birth date and current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is June 2nd, '08. And the age is-- nearly 88.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of your parents and any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No brothers, no sisters. My mother's name was Mathilde, born Vohryzek. And father was Emil Brod. He was the second youngest son in a big family. And mother was the oldest daughter in the big family, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's start by having you describe your early family life. What were your parents doing in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: The early family life actually is hard to describe because, as a little boy, I had rickets. So the doctor didn't permit me to walk at all. I was-- my locomotion was on one of those pot chambers. It was the only thing that I could do, because they were afraid my legs would be crooked until about three-- three-- three and a half years. Then I could start walking. Then-- from then on, I was more or less of a loner, because father was always busy. Mother tried to help her parents in selling-- her parents, that was part of their income. They had in those little Bohemian towns and villages, the farmers' women in winter had nothing to do. But they were very handy in needlepoint work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they bought the material, had it imprinted with what they wanted, the patterns on it. And then they farmed it out to those farmers' women over the winter, sometimes as much as 200 of them. And then afterwards, they had to sell it on their own.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, at that time, silk was still pretty unknown. You had only very fine linens and fine-- very fine cottons, instead. So that was-- they sold those to the girls who were to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was doing this, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I will come to him, then. Mother helped them in Vienna through her connections, because that helped the business. Father, at that time, he was-- how would I word it-- a piece man. He was cutting-- in-- sampling and cutting cloth for men's clothing. Because you had the one thing-- you didn't have-- you had some factories for clothing, but they were only for cheap clothing and anybody who wanted to be somebody wouldn't wear that. He went to a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What standard of living did your family have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle class, [INTERPOSING VOICES] always middle class.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of your memories as a-- as a child growing up in Vienna? What do you think back on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, comparatively little, because I was alone even then. There was no Jewish family around. And the boys-- there were very few boys in that neighborhood. And somehow or other, they were free, and I was not free. So they didn't find me congenial to them. So they broke my toys, and then they didn't come any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not free?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't too much free to-- to move. I could move around in the apartment. But most of the time, it was still at the time where I had to spend sitting more than walking. Only much later when I was about four years old, then I was more free again. There in the neighborhood there were not many young boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the-- you see, that is also something rather strange to you and funny when I think of it. Up to the age of 13 or 14, girls didn't exist for us. They were a minor, lower part of humanity. If we were boys, we wanted only to associate with boys-- fight and [INAUDIBLE] games and that. But girls didn't belong there. We didn't discover the girls until after 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, still when you think back to your early days in Vienna, take us back to that era. What-- what was it like growing up as a Jewish kid there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only--you got more or less a little bit of feedback, was a negative one. Because you lived among Christians, and most of them, even though they knew you were Jewish, it didn't matter-- because you didn't have this segregation there at all. It wasn't possible. Except one district in Vienna where the Jewish refugees from Galicia came during the war. And the poor ones-- they went into the former ghetto district. But the rest of the Jews, they lived all over Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much was your family tied up with the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all, except for the synagogue. And there the synagogue was not a synagogue like here [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: A congregation?

speaker: interviewee
text: A congregation. But it was on the state. It was a Jewish organization recognized by the state, and had a right to tax wealthy Jews and enforce the taxes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the synagogues were built by the Jewish community. The rabbis were installed by the Jewish community independent of what the members would have wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: It had to be approved by the Jewish community. And what you would call here probably traditional that was considered here more or less semi-orthodox. But on the other hand, for example, you didn't have any Jew having kippahs. That was not-- not known.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean to you to be Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a slight detriment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] By the nature of where we lived, I lived mostly among Jews-- among non-Jews. So I associated with non-Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was it a detriment to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, every so often, not so often, but every so seldom, being Jew broke through in their eyes. But it wasn't too often. Only later on, when the nationalist parties were inspired by Germany, started preaching nationalism. And that was connected with anti-Semitism. There was always an indigent anti-Semitism. But that was partly through the fault of the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of comments or attitudes did you pick up growing up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really much. You didn't hear too many of those dirty words like you would hear somewhere else. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They might have sometimes felt it, but they didn't put it into words. At least in the middle class. But the lower class is where I don't know. Because you had a very, very big segregation of classes there. It was definitely a class society that your imperial court, your high nobility, your middle nobility, your low nobility, then your upper middle class, patricians, middle class, lower middle class, and then the rest of the population was proletariat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, again, to stick to your story as much as possible and not get too far off into that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to school there. In school, I don't remember in the first class in Vienna of anti-Semitism. But then we moved to Bohemia because father was in the war. And there, the Czechs didn't like the Jews for one reason-- because the Germans and the Jews always stuck together. And most of the Jews among themselves spoke German and not Czech. And the Czechs were fanatic nationalists. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But one thing I will say for them-- it never, I shouldn't say never-- but in my time, it never gave place to pogroms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So growing up there, you didn't, there wasn't any violence at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, fights like among boys anyhow. And so fought back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you know that you were Jewish? What was it about your family that made it different from the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, naturally, mother celebrate-- held all the holidays. And Yom Kippur and New Year's even to the synagogue. I had to, naturally, in school, as well in Vienna as in Bohemia, because that was state, federal schools-- high schools-- there was compulsory religion. They had a priest come for the Catholics, a rabbi come in for the Jews, and the preacher for the Protestants. [BACKGROUND NOISE] That was by law. And they were paid by the law. So the religion didn't enter into the normal course of study at all. It was separate hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any hierarchy presented that the Jewish-- the Jewish people were higher or lower than anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any value associated with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nearly equal. That's the intention-- nearly equal. And-- but you had the segregation there. And I don't want to think that is my value judgment. But I just want to give you a picture. There was a resentment of the eastern Jews when they came to Vienna and Bohemia, too, because they brought with them practices that were absolutely taboo in Bohemia or in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about that. What was the difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bargaining, cheating, and those things. I don't mean to say that they all they did them. But the few who did it set their opinion of the-- of the majority. Like, for example, we knew a furrier, very orthodox, the two brothers. They had several furrier shops. They were not poor. And they were very religious. But afterwards, within a few years, they both went to jail for cheating. And things like that. And that, naturally, that is one thing that I still remember when first the anti-Semitism started before the Nazis came to Vienna. But they were in Germany. So the Viennese said, you Viennese Jews don't have anything to worry. We are only against the eastern Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to them, look, they will start maybe with the eastern Jews and end up with us. I never was bashful about uttering my opinions. Well, how did you, growing up, feel about the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was natural. They were not strange to me, because that was-- how should I say-- this was the society in which I lived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Naturally, certain of my interests varied from the others. But that was partly because of the family background and not of the Jewish background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were about 25 when-- when Hitler first came into power in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1932, I was at 32-- '08, so 24 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember starting to change--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Hitler started actually in 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, then let's-- let's go back earlier. What did you start--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was an unknown quantity. Nobody ever heard much of it, except from certain political partisanships with which we had nothing to do. You had, in Vienna and Austria, you could say not Bohemia, that was different altogether. You had, first of all, the Social Democrats comparable to the Democrats here. You had the Christian Social Party, which was Christian but Republican. And then you had the pan-German Party, which was a minor party. But because of the minority, they played a tong on the balance. They had more influence than their number would have dictated. So they somehow took over certain parts of education, especially high school. Those professors there were not teachers. They were professors. And they had belonged to the German professor union, not union, society let's call it. And there they were implanted with the German nationalism, because at that time, they didn't like Austria as a separate country. They wanted it to be part of Germany. On the other hand, the Social Democrats and the Christian Socialist didn't want that. They wanted it to remain Austria so they could obtain influence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were going to school, what kind of distortion or propaganda did you suspect you were getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, except for one man in high school that was in Vienna-- he was a Jesuit priest. But I'm just mentioning it. But it had nothing to do with it. We had one Jesuit priest there who was wonderful. He always went out of his way for me. But this man always, every third word when he tried to teach was the Masons and the Jews. So I finally thought, I have to put an end to it. I sat in the corner of the bench. So  I moved out and laughed in his face, loud.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a few times, he gave it up. Because he knew when he would complain about me, he would get fired, because that wouldn't be tolerated. Was this the general attitude in other teachers also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, they belong to the German Teacher's Society. So you had a certain influence on it. So that-- this was undeniable. But on the other hand, still, they were in a conflict. They treated me in a way as an equal and as a non-equal. Do I make myself clear there?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was always aware of it. Only, I put up a fight when it came to it. I didn't run away from it. Take, for example, in the first class of technical high school, we had two parallel classes. We were over 100. By the time we came to the seventh class, naturally some of them had moved out to other schools. But most of them dropped out at-- at the fourth class. We were only about 15 or 16 in the whole class who passed the maturity exam. It was a definite elitist system.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did this start to affect you and your family in some way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did these tendencies start to-- to handicap you or your family either personally, or professionally, or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, actually-- Not until the Nazis came into Vienna, because most of my friends and fellows were non-Jewish-- girls as well as boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: So until then, I felt very little of it, naturally. You know, and then I heard from somebody-- like, let's say I went with a girl when I was 16, 17, or so-- she said something about Jews. I said, what do you want? I'm Jewish. You don't want to go with me? Oh, you are different. What was the-- the value that she was telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not a value. It was actually more the-- how should I call it-- psychologically. It was a psychological, environmental effect.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was she saying about Jews that you're an exception to?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't say very much because I stopped her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a sense of what she was getting at though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, that was the Jew. You were also a human being. But not on the Viennese Jews, you see, because-- and [? that ?] was [? the ?] pity for the eastern Jews. They were exposed to all the negative aspects much more through than the Viennese Jews. That's why to me, later on, when Hitler-- he wasn't still in Austria, but in Germany-- some of the people I knew, students and so, who were belonging to the Nazi Party, which I found  out later. He said, to you Viennese Jews, nothing will happen-- we are only after the eastern Jews. I said, don't kid me. You'll start with them, and you'll end up with us. I was very outspoken about those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, again, when Hitler did start coming into power, what did you notice changing around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was altogether different, because it wasn't that so much that the population at large was anti-Semitic. But they were scared of it themselves, because at that time, many-- not all the teachers, but many a teacher in school, especially high school, and grammar school even more so, asked the-- the pupils what the parents spoke about. And then they reported it to the Gestapo. In each house, the Nazis installed-- the janitor had to be a Nazi member and had to report to the Gestapo what went on in the house. So it was a general fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of things would be reported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything that was not for the party. Anything, even if it had to be invented.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what? What specifically would be considered dangerous? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see-- It's really hard for me to tell, because I never was really exposed to it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if they were anti-Nazi. Let's put it that way. That probably the basic thing. And the majority of the population was not pro-Nazi. Actually, where you had most of the Nazi supporters was in the so-called educated classes, teaching and so forth-- not in the lawyers, not in the doctors, naturally. And then among the very low population, and the criminal population. They made a big-- Then one thing that made a lot of Nazi supporters was unemployment. The young fellows didn't-- couldn't find a job not even as a-- on a commission job. They were-- they were years without a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they started leaning towards--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to believe somebody. So it was the Jews-- the Jewish bankers which didn't exist over there at that time, except for the Rothschilds.

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly did Jewish people have to be afraid to say? What would they be reported for?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean before or after Hitler?

speaker: interviewer
text: After.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all of a sudden, they were not citizens. They were half tolerated. But they knew, more or less-- no, they didn't know. They thought it will blow over. But they knew that it was against them. And here I want to repeat what I told you before. Originally, so far as I had the impression traveling in those circles, they didn't mean to kill all the Jews. He wanted to get the Jews out of Europe, conquer Europe, and make it a German Europe. Actually, he said that time, look, today's Germans are no Germans. They are not racially pure. I would have to found a society that gradually purifies them-- some blond, blue-eyed girls and boys, put them into what they called schools, or whatever you want to call it, where they would create new Aryan children. The Aryan concept was the craziest thing to anybody who thought about it. Because the Aryans were some people in ancient India. And the Germans never came from India. The Aryans in India meant the noble ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when your-- When this attitude was becoming more and more pervasive, how did your family respond? Did anything change for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. For-- for example, one thing-- Father, even though he was Jewish, he was probably the only Jew in the tailor organization-- customs tailors. You see, that was still on the old principles built-- they were still, so to say, privileged. So he was the financial man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then later on, he said to have spoke to them. And most of them are Catholic socialists-- socials.

speaker: interviewee
text: He spoke to them. He wanted to relinquish the job. They said, no, stay with-- stay with it. He said, no, I don't belong any more to you. So he stopped right then. Naturally, when they came to power, they-- he had to close up shop. He couldn't use any of their material. They took an inventory of the materials. That was not any more ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- from that moment on, we felt it immediately, even though much of the population sympathized with us. But they didn't dare to show it too openly. Well, how did the people around you treat you personally? Did anything change?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same way. Only I was aware of the danger. Actually, there were a few miscreants in the neighborhood who tried to report on Jews. But basically, the population didn't go along with it. But they didn't dare to protest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the joke of it was, for example, that many of the top Nazis were of Slavic origin, not of German origin. They had typically Slavic names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, continuing historically, keep going through the 1930s. What are the memories that you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of the memories-- the more specific episodes that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, actually, since they came in '38 to Vienna, always you didn't know from one moment to the other what might happen. It was fear. Because if you're fully exposed to them, you didn't count. Whatever you said, it didn't count. If they lied, even that counted. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we automatically withdrew into a shell. Jewish sociability stopped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the coffee houses where Jews used to associate-- there were always spies around there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The janitor was a spy. You never knew who was a spy, too. Look there-- in the neighborhood, we had one Jewish family. They had a grocery store-- a very big nice one. The daughter married. Afterwards, he was a Nazi. And she was Jewish. It's such contradictions-- senseless contradictions there. And to a great extent, it may sound funny to you, but to a great extent, it was the girls. They liked the uniform. They told their boyfriends you should join-- they have a nice uniform. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It sounds all silly. But psychologically, it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you start experiencing danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I knew that once it happened, I knew it was over. I still worked until they kept me there until June. I was-- there was only one more Jew in the firm that was still tolerated there. And then when the Nuremberg Laws were put in force, then the director-- the new director called me and said, I'm sorry, you were a decent fellow here. But, that's the law, we have to let you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a-- a cost accountant administrator.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when exactly were you let go? When did that law take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: That started in June '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: So until 1938, your life was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Look, whenever I was in the streetcar or in the street I was in danger that one of the Nazis would not arrest me, but take me with him. We were just, how should I word it, free booty.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would people know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, first of all, there was a slight differentiation. To them, I looked Jewish. To others, I didn't. It depends on your perception.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but I never tried to hide it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If somebody would ask me, naturally, I'm a Jew. I was, in a way, a Jew by default. But you didn't dress differently or have any-- you didn't have the yellow stars? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at that time, yet. Like even in the concentration camp, we still didn't have the numbers. That came out later. And the concentration camp was a final experience. You didn't know from one moment to the other what they could do and would do. Yeah, we were Jews, the lowest scum of them all. We were a minority in the camp. You had a lot of other people-- gypsies and other people.
